Brugg

AGAargau

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The Roman legionary's lost letter

A child finds a wax tablet near the ruins of Vindonissa and, by reading the Latin words aloud, is transported back to 70 AD. They must help a young Roman soldier deliver his urgent message home before the legion marches away to the Danube -- racing through the camp's amphitheatre, barracks, and underground aqueducts.

Create This Story

Vindonissa (today Windisch, adjacent to Brugg) was the only permanent Roman legionary camp in Switzerland, housing approximately 6,000 soldiers. Three legions were stationed here in succession: Legio XIII Gemina, Legio XXI Rapax, and Legio XI Claudia. The Vindonissa Museum in Brugg displays the largest collection of Roman writing tablets anywhere -- 612 pine-wood tablets found in rubbish dumps, each a wax-covered diptych on which soldiers wrote letters, receipts, and legal documents with a stylus.

The bridge that built a dynasty

The story of why Brugg exists: a child discovers that the narrow Aare crossing -- just 12 metres wide -- was so important that whoever controlled it could become king. Told from the perspective of young Radbot building his castle on the hill above, dreaming of a dynasty that would one day rule half of Europe.

Create This Story

Brugg sits at the narrowest point of the Aare in the Swiss Mittelland, where the river squeezes through a 200-metre gorge and narrows to just 12 metres -- the only spot between Lake Thun and the Rhine where the Aare could be crossed with a single log. Around 1020-1030, nobleman Radbot and Bishop Werner of Strasbourg built Habsburg Castle on the Wuelpelsberg hill, 3 km southwest of Brugg. Radbot's grandson Otto II first called himself 'Count of Habsburg.' In 1273, his descendant Rudolf was elected King of the Romans.

The Black Tower's secret stones

The Schwarze Turm whispers at night because its walls contain stones quarried from the Roman camp of Vindonissa. A child follows the whispers and meets a Roman centurion trapped between two eras -- his stones now holding up a medieval tower he does not recognise. Together they must reconcile two thousand years of history locked in the same wall.

Create This Story

The Schwarze Turm (Black Tower) is Brugg's landmark and oldest building, built in the last quarter of the 12th century, probably by Count Albrecht III of Habsburg. It served as a fortified bridgehead guarding the narrow Aare crossing. Builders reused stone from the ruins of Vindonissa, which for centuries led people to believe the tower was of Roman origin -- this misconception was corrected only in the 19th century. The tower is 25.70 metres tall with a 9.30 by 9.30 metre ground plan.

The three rivers' promise

At the Wasserschloss, where Aare, Reuss, and Limmat unite, a water spirit from each river argues over which is the mightiest. The Aare claims the longest journey, the Reuss the wildest gorges, the Limmat the greatest city. A child must help them see that only together -- carrying water from 40% of Switzerland -- are they truly strong.

Create This Story

Just downstream from Brugg, the three great rivers from the Alps -- Aare, Reuss, and Limmat -- unite in a single spot called the 'Wasserschloss der Schweiz' (Water Castle of Switzerland). Their combined discharge averages 555 cubic metres per second, draining water from 40% of Switzerland's total area. In 1989, Canton Aargau protected this floodplain landscape; it is designated a wetland of national importance and is rich in biodiversity.

The queen's windows

Agnes of Hungary hides a secret message in the stained glass of Koenigsfelden -- a message about forgiveness for her father's murder. Centuries later, a child notices that when sunlight hits the windows in a certain order, the panels tell a story only visible to those who look with an open heart.

Create This Story

On 1 May 1308, King Albrecht I of Habsburg was murdered by his nephew Johann von Schwaben near Windisch. His widow Elisabeth founded Koenigsfelden Monastery at the murder site around 1310-1311. Their daughter Agnes of Hungary presided over its golden age from 1317 to 1364, commissioning magnificent stained glass windows created between 1320-1360. These windows are considered, together with those of Bern Minster, the most valuable medieval stained glass in Switzerland. The windows were donated by the murdered king's family members, whose images appear in the panels.

The amphitheater's last performance

Eleven thousand seats sit empty in the Vindonissa amphitheater. A child discovers that on the anniversary of the last legion's departure in 101 AD, ghostly legionaries return for one final spectacle -- not gladiatorial combat, but a farewell ceremony where 6,000 soldiers say goodbye to the land that was their home.

Create This Story

The amphitheatre of Vindonissa was the largest Roman amphitheater in Switzerland: outer dimensions 111 by 99 metres, arena 64 by 52 metres, seating for approximately 11,000 spectators. It was originally built in wood, later rebuilt in stone with three concentric elliptical walls around 50 AD. The final legion, Legio XI Claudia, was withdrawn by Emperor Trajan to the Danube region in 101 AD, leaving the camp and its amphitheatre empty.

The Little Prophet Town

A child attending the old Latin School in Brugg discovers that the allegorical paintings on its 1640 facade come alive during exams, offering cryptic advice -- in Latin -- to students brave enough to ask. Each mural holds a lesson from a different century of Brugg's scholarly tradition, and the child must decipher them all to pass the ultimate test.

Create This Story

Brugg's Lateinschule (Latin School) is a heritage site of national significance: the northern part dates from 1515 (late Gothic), the southern from 1638/40 (Baroque). The east facade features architectural-allegorical murals by Rudolf Schwerter from 1640. The school served as a preparatory school for the theological academy in Bern, funded by income from the dissolved Koenigsfelden monastery. It produced so many pastors and scholars that Brugg earned the nickname 'Prophetenstaedtchen' (Little Prophet Town).

The light beam from PSI

During a family visit to PSI, a child accidentally steps into the path of the Swiss Light Source beam and discovers they can suddenly see the invisible world of atoms. Everything in Brugg -- the old Roman stones, the river water, the medieval glass -- is alive at the smallest scale, and the child sees how 2,000 years of history are connected atom by atom.

Create This Story

The Paul Scherrer Institute (PSI), located near Brugg, is the largest research institute for natural and engineering sciences in Switzerland. Named after physicist Paul Scherrer (1890-1969), who co-developed the Debye-Scherrer method and helped establish CERN. PSI employs 2,300 people and hosts 3,000 visiting researchers annually. It operates the Swiss Light Source (synchrotron), SwissFEL (X-ray laser), and Switzerland's only proton therapy facility for precise cancer treatment.
